prompt: Explain what a "closure" is in programming, in exactly one sentence. The sentence can be long.
response: A closure is a function that, when executed, has a persistent private "lexical" (or "static") environment that "captures" (or "closes over") the values of its free (non-local) variables from the environment in which it was "bound" (or "defined") at the time of its "creation" (or "declaration"), so that the function can later "access" (or "use") those values in its "body" (or "scope") even when the function is "called" (or "invoked") in another environment, effectively "binding" (or "closing") the captured values in the function's "scope" (or "closure"), hence the name "closure" (or "lexical closure").
